title: ⚔️ Chapter Boss ⭐⭐⭐: The Subjunctive and Unreal Past

options.text: I suggest that he take a different approach.
It is necessary that all documents be signed.
The manager demanded that she works overtime.
We recommend that the policy be reviewed annually.

questions.explanation: The error is (c): after demand + that, the subjunctive base form is required — she work, not she works. (a) take, (b) be signed, and (d) be reviewed are all correct subjunctive forms (bare base, no -s, no auxiliary).

questions.prompt: Complete: "___ this information to reach the press, the consequences would be catastrophic."

options.text: Should
Were
Had
Would

questions.explanation: The full non-inverted version is "If this information were to reach the press…" — an unreal future condition using were to. The inverted form drops if and puts Were first: Were this information to reach… "Should" inverts "If…should"; "Had" inverts past perfect; "Would" cannot invert.

questions.prompt:
Read: "If only I had revised more thoroughly, I would have passed the exam."
Choose the EQUIVALENT sentence.

options.text: Had I revised more thoroughly, I would have passed the exam.
Should I revise more thoroughly, I will pass the exam.
Were I to revise more thoroughly, I would pass the exam.
I'd rather I revised more thoroughly to pass the exam.

questions.explanation: "If only I had revised" is a past-perfect unreal condition; the inverted equivalent is "Had I revised more thoroughly…" with the same result clause. (b) is a future possibility (wrong tense relationship). (c) is an unreal future, not past. (d) misuses I'd rather.

questions.prompt: Complete: "I'd sooner we ___ the meeting until next week."

options.text: postpone
will postpone
postponed
had postponed

questions.explanation: "I'd sooner" (= I'd rather) followed by a different subject takes the past tense: I'd sooner we postponed. The present tense postpone, the future will postpone, and the past perfect had postponed are all incorrect in this fixed structure.

questions.prompt:
Which option correctly transforms the sentence into a formal inverted conditional?
"If there should be any questions, please contact our helpdesk."

options.text: Were there any questions, please contact our helpdesk.
Had there been any questions, please contact our helpdesk.
Would there be any questions, please contact our helpdesk.
Should there be any questions, please contact our helpdesk.

questions.explanation: "If there should be" inverts to "Should there be" — keeping the present/future meaning. (a) Were there inverts a were-conditional (unreal present); (b) Had there been inverts a past perfect (past unreal); (c) Would cannot invert. Only (d) is the correct transformation.

questions.prompt: Complete with the most formal and grammatically precise option: "It is crucial that the witness ___ the truth and nothing but the truth."

options.text: will tell
tell
tells
should tell

questions.explanation: The adjective crucial triggers the mandative subjunctive: the witness tell (bare base form). "Will tell" is a future indicative, "tells" is third-person present indicative (adds -s, which is wrong in the subjunctive), and "should tell" is an informal alternative — grammatically acceptable but not the purest subjunctive form expected at C2.
